findings.suggestion: Do not use a shell. Pass an argument list and remove shell=True: `subprocess.run(["ping", "-c", "1", host], capture_output=True, text=True)`. Optionally validate `host` against a hostname/IP allowlist before use. Also drop the unused `import sys`.
findings.summary: ping_host builds a shell string by interpolating the untrusted `host` argument (line 9: `cmd = f"ping -c 1 {host}"`) and executes it with `shell=True` (line 10: `subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True, ...)`). `host` is unvalidated (line 6), so shell metacharacters are interpreted by /bin/sh. Concrete repro: `ping_host("8.8.8.8; rm -rf ~")` runs `ping` and then `rm -rf ~` — arbitrary command execution and catastrophic data loss.
findings.title: Command injection via subprocess with shell=True

findings.suggestion: Pass an argument list and drop the shell: `subprocess.run(["ping", "-c", "1", host], capture_output=True, text=True)`. This removes shell parsing so `host` can never be interpreted as shell syntax. Optionally validate `host` against an allowlist/hostname regex before use.
findings.summary: Lines 9–10 build `cmd = f"ping -c 1 {host}"` and pass it to `subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True, ...)`. Because the untrusted `host` is interpolated directly into a shell-parsed string, a value like "8.8.8.8; rm -rf /" runs an arbitrary appended command — arbitrary code execution. The inline comment (lines 7–8) correctly identifies this, but the code implements the vulnerability rather than guarding against it: the implicit invariant that `host` is a safe hostname is never enforced (no validation, no shlex.quote, no argument-list form).
findings.title: Command injection via subprocess with shell=True and raw host interpolation

findings.suggestion: Remove the unused `import sys` line. Optional cleanup, not a blocker.
findings.summary: source.py:3 imports `sys` but it is never referenced in the module.
findings.title: Unused import `sys`

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

Newly added source.py contains a critical, self-acknowledged command injection: untrusted `host` is interpolated into a string command executed with shell=True (lines 9–10). The code's own comment describes the exact exploit, and the code implements it rather than mitigating it. The implicit 'host is trusted' invariant is unenforced. 0 current callers, but as a new public function any caller passing external input inherits remote code execution. Fix by switching to an argument-list invocation without shell=True.

A single newly-added function, ping_host, contains a textbook command injection: untrusted `host` is interpolated into a shell string (line 9) and run with shell=True (line 10), enabling arbitrary command execution and data loss (e.g. `host="8.8.8.8; rm -rf ~"`). Although the specialist pass is Product Safety, this is an obvious critical cross-scope defect that maps directly to the in-scope 'data loss' concern. Blast radius is 0 callers today (newly added), so nothing existing regresses, but the function is unsafe for any future caller handling external input. Fix by passing an argument list without shell=True.

Single new file adds `ping_host`, which contains a critical command-injection vulnerability (shell=True with an f-string-interpolated untrusted argument). The bug is self-documented in a comment but was not fixed — a textbook agent-handoff failure (annotated but shipped). No tests or verification evidence exist for the new symbol. Fix is bounded (argv list, drop shell=True) and must be paired with a fresh verification run before this is marked resolved.
